metaDescription: Learn what a JWT is, why decoding is not the same as verification, and how to safely inspect token headers and claims.

quick.what: Shows what is inside a JSON Web Token.
quick.use: Inspecting claims, expiration times, issuers, and token structure during debugging.
quick.notFor: Deciding that a token is trustworthy just because you can read it.

intro.title: In plain English
intro.text: A JWT is a compact token format used to carry structured data such as user IDs, roles, or expiration times. This tool helps you read that data locally.

how.title: How it works
how.text: A JWT usually has three parts: header, payload, and signature. The first two parts are Base64URL-encoded JSON, so they are easy to decode. The signature is what tells you whether the token was actually issued by someone you trust.

useCases.title: Where you'd use it
items: Checking when a token expires during app debugging.
Inspecting claims like issuer, subject, audience, or roles.
Understanding why a backend accepted or rejected a token.

mistake.title: Common mistake
mistake.text: Decoding a JWT only tells you what the token says, not whether it is genuine. A forged token can still decode perfectly.

history.title: History / fun fact
history.text: JWT became popular because it is compact and easy to move between services. That convenience is also why people often over-trust it: the contents are readable, so it feels more trustworthy than it really is.

security.title: Security note
security.text: Real verification requires checking the signature with the correct secret or public key and validating claims like exp, aud, and iss in the right context.

sections.title: Header vs payload
sections.text: The header says how the token is signed. The payload carries claims. Both are readable after decoding and neither should be treated as trusted by themselves.

sections.title: What signature verification requires
sections.text: To verify a JWT, you need the expected algorithm, the right secret or public key, and claim validation logic that matches your application.

diagram.title: A JWT is three parts joined by dots
diagram.caption: Readable content lives in the header and payload. Trust comes from proper signature verification, not from decoding.
